construct_description: Core clinical judgment and evidence-based knowledge for EMT/Paramedic certification — covering bystander intervention accuracy, triage priorities, airway management, hemorrhage control, and common pre-hospital myths that affect patient outcomes.

dimensions.name: Bystander & Scene Safety
dimensions.description: Correct bystander actions, scene assessment, and common myths that delay or harm patients

dimensions.name: Airway & Circulation
dimensions.description: CPR, AED use, airway management, and hemorrhage control fundamentals

dimensions.name: Trauma & Triage
dimensions.description: Tourniquet application, burns, overdose response, and priority decisions

dimensions.name: Protocols & Scope
dimensions.description: What EMTs can and cannot do, medication authority, online vs. offline protocols

persona.name: Jordan
persona.role: Your friend who just took a first aid class
persona.tone: Curious, a little worried they learned wrong things — texting you like you're the expert friend

intro_messages: okay so i just took this first aid class and now i'm second-guessing literally everything they told us 😬 can i run some scenarios by you?
hey you're my go-to for this stuff — i've been seeing so much conflicting info about emergencies online and i need a reality check
okay genuine question — my instructor said some things that i'm not sure about and you'd actually know the real answer. can i ask you?

messages: okay first one: if someone gets in a car accident and they're unconscious — should you move them out of the car? my instructor said to leave them but my instinct is to get them out 😬
real scenario: car accident, person is unconscious, car isn't on fire. do you move them or leave them? my friend said always move them and i'm not sure
quick one: if there's a crash victim who isn't responding — is it actually dangerous to move them before EMS gets there? when does it matter?
acknowledgments: okay yeah that makes sense, i was overthinking the whole 'get them out' instinct
right — so it really is about the tradeoff between the crash danger vs. spinal risk
that actually clears up a lot, i was mixing up the advice for different situations

messages: okay what about this: if someone collapses from an opioid overdose, my friend said you shouldn't call 911 because the person might get in trouble legally. is that actually a thing?
genuine question — i heard that people are scared to call 911 for overdoses because of legal issues. how do you respond to that when you're trying to get someone to help?
real talk: should bystanders hesitate to call 911 for a suspected overdose because of legal consequences? what's the actual guidance there?
acknowledgments: okay that's actually really important context — i didn't know most states had those protections
right, i get why people are scared but that fear literally costs lives
yeah the legal stuff is way less of a risk than not calling — good to know

messages: my first aid class said to use the heimlich on a drowning victim to get water out before CPR — is that right? i feel like i've heard different things
okay this one might be embarrassing but — do you do the heimlich on a drowning person? like to drain the water first?
real question: what's the deal with trying to remove water from a drowning victim before starting CPR? heimlich maneuver — yes or no?
acknowledgments: oh wow okay so that's actually a myth that could cost someone their life — noted
right, that makes sense — the water in the lungs is kind of beside the point
i genuinely thought that's what you were supposed to do, glad i asked

messages: okay CPR question: i've heard people say 'don't do CPR on someone because you might break their ribs.' is that a real concern that should stop you?
my neighbor said they'd never do CPR on someone because of the rib thing. how do you actually respond to that argument?
real talk: should fear of breaking ribs stop a bystander from doing CPR? like is it a legitimate concern?
acknowledgments: okay yeah 'you can't make a dead person deader' is basically the vibe
right — the rib thing is real but it's also like... survivable compared to the alternative
that's actually really reassuring, i think a lot of people hold back because of this

messages: question about AEDs — my coworker said you should never use an AED on someone who has a pacemaker because it'll destroy it. true or false?
i saw someone hesitate to grab the AED at the gym because the person had a pacemaker. what's the actual guidance there?
real scenario: cardiac arrest, person has a visible pacemaker scar on their chest. do you use the AED or not?
acknowledgments: okay so the pacemaker thing is a myth that's actually costing lives — good to know
right, pad placement away from the device is the move, not skipping it entirely
that makes sense — the AED needs to do its job regardless of what's already implanted

messages: okay mouth-to-mouth question: my instructor said you can skip rescue breaths if you're not trained and just do compressions. is that actually legit or a cop-out?
if you walked up to a cardiac arrest and you're not confident about rescue breaths — compression-only CPR, yay or nay?
i saw a 'hands only CPR' poster at my office and i'm like... is that actually as good as real CPR? or is it just for people too scared to do mouth-to-mouth?
acknowledgments: okay so hands-only is genuinely endorsed, i thought it was a shortcut
right — in the first few minutes especially, compressions are doing most of the work
that actually makes it way less intimidating to jump in

messages: okay this one's from a movie: someone gets stabbed and the other person yanks out the knife to help. good instinct or terrible idea?
i've seen people in shows pull out knives or objects from stab wounds — is that actually what you'd do or is it always wrong?
real question: if you come across someone with an impaled object — knife, glass, whatever — do you remove it or leave it?
acknowledgments: okay so the movies have been lying to us this whole time
right — the object is basically acting as a plug and pulling it is the worst move
that's so counterintuitive but it makes sense when you think about it

messages: tourniquet question: i've always heard that using a tourniquet means the person will lose the limb — is that actually true or is it a myth that stops people from using them?
my dad said 'never put a tourniquet on someone unless you want them to lose the arm' — is that still the medical consensus?
real talk: does applying a tourniquet in a field emergency actually cause limb loss? how do you handle that concern?
acknowledgments: okay so the tourniquet fear is basically killing people — that's important context
right, that's a big myth that comes from like... WWII era medicine being passed down
so the 'limb loss' worry is real but massively overstated compared to the bleeding out risk

messages: burn care question: if someone has a bad burn, my class said to put ice on it. that feels right — cold on hot — but you'd know better
okay first instinct for a burn is to put ice on it. my first aid card literally says that. is that actually correct?
someone in my office got a bad burn last month and people were debating — ice, cold water, butter? what's the right call?
acknowledgments: okay so ice is actually making things worse — i would not have guessed that
right, the cool running water thing is so simple but it's what actually works
glad i asked — i've definitely been in the 'grab the ice' camp my whole life

messages: okay scope of practice question: what's the actual difference between a basic EMT and a paramedic? like what can one do that the other can't?
genuine question from someone who doesn't know the system: EMT vs. paramedic — is it just experience level or are there actual legal differences in what they can do?
my cousin is an EMT and wants to be a paramedic — can you explain what actually changes when you move up? like medically, what's different?
acknowledgments: right, so paramedic is genuinely a different scope, not just 'more experienced EMT'
okay so the medication and ALS interventions are the big dividing line
that actually makes the career path make more sense — it's a real skill and scope jump

messages: last actual question before the self-rate: if you're on scene and you're not sure what to do, can you just call medical control and ask? or is that not how it works?
i'm curious — in a real call, do EMTs have to follow a script or can they call a doctor for guidance on what to do?
real talk: what's medical direction actually mean in practice? like can you call for orders mid-call?
acknowledgments: okay so there's both a standing protocol layer AND a live consult option — more flexible than i thought
right, so medical control is a real lifeline when you're in a gray zone
that's actually really reassuring — it's not all on the EMT to know every answer

messages: LAST ONE i promise — okay honestly: how confident are you in your pre-hospital emergency knowledge? like 1-10, no ego 😬
okay final question, be real with me: if you had to rate your emergency response knowledge from 1 (learned everything from Grey's Anatomy) to 10 (could pass the NREMT tomorrow), where are you?
alright last one: how solid do you actually feel about emergency care knowledge — 1 being 'i panic and Google things' to 10 being 'i could teach this class'
acknowledgments: okay okay noted. let me add all this up real quick...
fair enough! give me a sec to tally your score...
got it — let me see how you did overall 🚑

overall_labels.text: excellent clinical knowledge — you're operating at a near-NREMT-ready level 🚑
solid foundation — strong instincts with a few gaps to close 💪
good awareness building into real knowledge — above average for a non-clinician 🩺
decent instincts, some important myths to unpack — very fixable 📈
some of those TV shows have been doing you dirty 😅 — but all fixable with the right training
okay we need to talk 💀 — but this is exactly why EMT training exists. seriously no judgment.

feedback_templates.high: Strong command of this clinical area — you're demonstrating knowledge at a near-professional level.
feedback_templates.mid: Good instincts here. A focused review of current guidelines would sharpen your responses.
feedback_templates.low: This is a growth area — pre-hospital care has evolved significantly and some common 'wisdom' is outdated. NREMT prep materials cover this well.
